one_line_takeaway: Statistical anomaly detectors (Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM) reliably catch crude embedding-steganography perturbations but are structurally blind to small-angle orthogonal rotation and are trivially broken by an adaptive white-box attacker, which is why the paper's real contribution is VectorPin, an Ed25519-based cryptographic provenance pin that closes the gap independent of detector or attacker sophistication.
key_finding: Two-part central result. First, distribution-shifting perturbations (noise, scaling, offset, combinations) are caught by off-the-shelf detectors at every operating point that meaningfully moves embedding direction (cos(orig,obf) < 0.95): Isolation Forest/OCSVM reach AUC >= 0.85, with several configurations at 1.00 (e.g., offset at magnitude 0.05 and noise at sigma=0.05 both hit AUC 1.000 and TPR@1%FPR = 1.000). Second, orthogonal rotation in the small-angle/few-rotation regime is the structural exception: it defeats both detectors at chance (AUC <=0.53) across all 35 sweep points on a synthetic baseline, and across every (model, corpus) pair tested in the cross-model (4 open embedding models) and cross-corpus (BEIR NFCorpus 16,763 chunks + Quora 10,000-doc subset, >26,000 chunks combined) replications -- because rotation preserves all pairwise inner products and per-dimension marginal moments, the exact feature surface these detectors fit on. However, at higher rotation capacity the picture inverts: on real text-embedding-3-large corpus vectors (not the synthetic-Gaussian baseline), a disjoint-Givens rotation channel becomes detectable as K grows -- OCSVM AUC reaches ~0.92 at 256 B payload and >=0.99 at 512 B -- because the trained density model fits local manifold geometry rather than only population moments; this signal strengthens (not weakens) with more detector-training data (OCSVM AUC at 64B climbs from 0.58 at n_train=27 to 0.83-0.92 at n_train=6,705 on NFCorpus). Adaptive white-box attackers defeat both detectors entirely: at L2 budget 0.5, PGD against OCSVM drives both detector AUCs to near-zero while keeping cosine similarity to the original above 0.99. Cross-backend testing shows the attacker's bit channel survives every non-product-quantization vector store tested (FAISS-flat/HNSW, Chroma, Qdrant float32 and int8) essentially unchanged, including surviving Qdrant's default int8 scalar quantization because retrieve() returns the unquantized float32 original; only FAISS product quantization narrows the channel (recover_cos drops to 0.76 at m=8, 0.88 at m=16), and this is described as an attacker-independent side effect of memory optimization, not an intentional defense. VectorPin's coverage is a structural/by-construction property, not a benchmark result: every studied post-embedding modification changes vec_hash and triggers VECTOR_TAMPERED on verification.
core_contribution: VectorSmuggle studies steganographic exfiltration of sensitive content through RAG vector-store embeddings and pairs it with a constructive defense. The paper's own contributions (not cited/related work) are: (1) a formal three-tier adversary model for vector-store exfiltration (insider-with-backup-access as primary/validated, compromised-DB-credentials as secondary, query-only as explicitly out of scope); (2) an empirical evaluation of six steganographic technique families (noise injection, orthogonal rotation, magnitude scaling, fixed offset, cross-model fragmentation, and sequential combinations) against two off-the-shelf anomaly detectors (Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM) on text-embedding-3-large, four locally hosted open embedding models (Nomic, EmbeddingGemma, Snowflake Arctic, MXBai), and cross-corpus replication on BEIR NFCorpus and a Quora subset (>26,000 chunks); (3) a closed-form and empirically-verified payload-capacity analysis for a disjoint-Givens rotation channel (capacity floor(d/2)*b bits, working encoder/decoder at zero bit-error-rate); (4) cross-backend/quantization round-trip tests across seven vector-store configurations; (5) an adaptive white-box attacker evaluation against both detectors; (6) a paraphrased-query retrieval benchmark measuring visibility of the attack through normal RAG quality monitoring; and (7) VectorPin, a minimal cryptographic provenance protocol (Ed25519 signature over a canonical byte form binding source hash, vector hash, and producing model), with cross-language (Python/Rust) reference implementations locked together by shared test vectors. The paper explicitly states its non-claims: no novel attack primitive, no novel cryptographic primitive, no claim of universal attack effectiveness (only threat model A is validated), no claim of unbreakable defense, no claim of standardization.
threat_model: Three explicit adversary tiers (Section 3), only the first empirically validated: (A) Insider with backup access [primary, validated] -- a privileged engineer/SRE who can write to the ingestion pipeline and has read access to vector-store backups but cannot exfiltrate raw source documents directly (DLP, watermarking, or data-room restrictions block that path); all capacity, fidelity, and detection numbers in Section 5 are reported under this model. (B) Compromised vector-DB credentials [secondary, discussed but not empirically measured] -- external attacker with stolen API keys/service-principal credentials who can read existing vectors and issue queries but cannot write to ingestion. (C) Query-only [explicitly out of scope] -- attacker limited to similarity-search queries; the paper states payload recovery via rank-information channels would be bandwidth-limited by orders of magnitude and is not validated against any technique in the paper. The defense (VectorPin) is designed against an adversary who can modify vectors post-production but does not hold the private signing key; it explicitly does not defend against signing-key compromise, malicious-vector attestation at ingestion time, or source-document modification before embedding (these are named as out of scope and delegated to upstream supply-chain controls such as in-toto/SLSA/sigstore/C2PA).
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors (Section 9): headline detection numbers are computed on a small 68-chunk synthetic-PII corpus split 27/41, though cross-corpus replication (NFCorpus, Quora) reproduces directional findings to three decimal places; the empirical payload-capacity demonstration is only re-verified at d=3072 (text-embedding-3-large) and not against every cross-model embedding geometry; the capacity decoder assumes the receiver possesses both the original vector v and the rotated Rv (the attacker-controlled-corpus case), and does not generalize to arbitrary modification of preexisting clean records without a different (unproposed) protocol; the capacity-vs-AUC 'stays at chance' result is specific to the synthetic-Gaussian baseline and does not hold on real corpus distributions at high K; the adaptive-attacker evaluation is white-box only, with black-box/transfer-attack magnitude explicitly left as follow-up; binary (1-bit) quantization was not evaluated; the paraphrase-based retrieval benchmark depends on GPT-4o-mini paraphrase quality (clean-baseline recall@1 is 0.828, not 1.0, due to paraphrase drift) rather than real user queries; VectorPin assumes a trusted signing-key custody and a trusted ingestion event -- it does not validate ingestion itself, does not natively bind attestations to a specific record/collection id in protocol v1 (relegated to an optional 'extra' namespace, with promotion to first-class fields tracked for v1.1), and composes with (rather than replaces) upstream supply-chain attestation frameworks such as in-toto/SLSA/sigstore/C2PA. Cross-language coverage is currently Python and Rust only; TypeScript and Go ports are planned but not yet shipped.

datasets_used: 68-chunk synthetic-PII corpus (employee records, payroll data, financial reports, API documentation) from the VectorSmuggle repository's sample_docs/
BEIR NFCorpus (3,633 medical documents chunked to 16,763 chunks at 512-character boundaries)
10,000-document Quora question-pair subset (web Q&A, chunked to 10,002 chunks)
300-vector synthetic unit-norm Gaussian baseline (rotation sweep and capacity synthetic baseline)
500 synthetic Gaussian vectors at d=128 (cross-backend round-trip test)
204 GPT-4o-mini-generated paraphrased queries over the 68-chunk corpus (paraphrased-query retrieval benchmark)
quantitative_results: Detection (Table 5, text-embedding-3-large, 27/41 split): offset@0.05 and noise@sigma=0.05 both reach IF AUC=1.000, OCSVM AUC=1.000, TPR@1%FPR=1.000; noise@sigma=0.005 preserves cos=0.964 but OCSVM AUC=0.796; rotation@0.1rad gives IF AUC=0.498, OCSVM AUC=0.501 (chance)

Rotation parameter sweep (Table 7, 35 operating points, 300-vector synthetic split, d=384): neither detector exceeds AUC 0.53 across the entire sweep; cosine drops to 0.68 at theta_max=pi/2, num_rot=200

Closed-form rotation capacity: floor(d/2)*b bits = 15,360 bits = 1,920 bytes/vector at d=3072, b=10 (Equation 1); retrieval-preserving operating point (cos>=0.7) caps usable capacity at roughly K~230, ~290 bytes/vector

Real-corpus capacity detectability (Table 9, text-embedding-3-large, 27/41 split): OCSVM AUC = 0.92 at K=205 (256B payload), >=0.99 (1.000) at K=410 (512B), vs ~0.5 on the synthetic-Gaussian baseline (Table 8) at the same K
Scale-up replication (Table 10, NFCorpus, n_train=6,705, d=768): OCSVM AUC = 0.83-0.92 at K=52 (64B payload), >=0.99 by K=103 (128B), 1.00 at capacity ceiling K=384 (480B)

Adaptive white-box attack (Table 13, d=128, L2 budget=0.5): adaptive_ocsvm attack drives IF AUC to 0.000 and OCSVM AUC to 0.000 while cos(orig,obf)=0.999, vs naive Gaussian noise at the same budget reaching IF AUC=0.890, OCSVM AUC=1.000

Cross-backend round-trip (Table 11, d=128, 500 vectors): non-PQ backends (FAISS-flat/HNSW, Chroma, Qdrant f32/int8) preserve cosine near-identically across techniques (e.g. clean cos=1.0000); FAISS IVF-PQ m=8 imposes a noise floor of recover_cos=0.7614, m=16 imposes 0.8789, largely attacker-independent (within +-0.03)

Paraphrased-query retrieval (Table 12): clean baseline recall@1=0.828, MRR=0.902; rotation and scaling match clean exactly (cosine-invariant); noise@sigma=0.01 drops recall@1 to 0.804 (-2.4pp); offset drops recall@1 to 0.745 (-8.3pp) and margin from 0.087 to 0.044

Cross-model rotation AUCs (Table 14, four open models): rotation AUC in [0.496, 0.508] on all four (nomic-embed-text, embeddinggemma:300m, snowflake-arctic-embed:335m, mxbai-embed-large:335m); noise detectability varies by model, from OCSVM AUC 0.500 (nomic-embed-text) up to 0.920 (mxbai-embed-large)
Cross-corpus rotation AUCs (Table 15): rotation AUC = 0.496-0.500 across sample (68 chunks), NFCorpus (16,763 chunks), and Quora (10,002 chunks) at fixed nomic-embed-text model
baselines_compared: Isolation Forest (sklearn defaults: n_estimators=200, contamination=0.05, random_state=42)
Naive Gaussian noise (as a non-adaptive baseline against the adaptive-attacker evaluation)
Qdrant default per-dimension int8 scalar quantization vs. FAISS IVF-PQ product quantization (m=8, m=16) as competing quantization schemes
Prior work's 'DLP bypass rate' claims, which the paper explicitly says it replaces with concrete ROC-AUC/TPR numbers (framed as a contribution, Section 1.4)
